data.title: Valero to import up to 6.5M barrels of Venezuelan crude in March - Reuters
data.description:
Valero Energy (VLO) is poised to buy as many as 6.5M barrels of Venezuelan crude in March bound for its Gulf Coast refineries, which could make it the top foreign refiner of Venezuelan oil since the U.S. captured President Maduro in January, Reuters reported Friday.

If Valero (VLO) succeeds in acquiring 10 or more cargoes next month, equivalent to ~210K bbl/day, the company could surpass Chevron (CVX) as the top U.S. refiner of Venezuelan crude, at least for now.

Chevron (CVX), the only U.S. major producing oil in Venezuela, is expected to raise exports of Venezuelan crude to ~300K bbl/day in March from 220K bbl/day in January, sources told Reuters last month.

Chevron (CVX) typically refines up to half of those exports at its own refineries and sells the rest to other U.S. refiners; much of Chevron's sales of Venezuelan oil to U.S. refiners typically go to Valero (VLO), and Chevron is expected to supply Valero with most of the volume the refiner is planning to import in March, according to the report.

Valero (VLO), which has the second-largest U.S. refining network capable of processing Venezuelan heavy oil, had a long-term supply agreement to buy crude from the country before the U.S. first sanctioned the country's oil industry in January 2019.

Valero's (VLO) total refining capacity for Venezuelan crude oil totaled ~240K bbl/day before a 2023 expansion at its 435K bbl/day refinery in Port Arthur, Texas, and the company has said it is now capable of processing a much larger volume of Venezuelan oil.

data.title: Exxon can sue California AG for defamation over recycling, judge rules
data.description:
A U.S. district judge on Friday rejected California Attorney General Rob Bonta's request to dismiss Exxon Mobil's (XOM) lawsuit accusing him of defamation in criticizing the company's initiatives around advanced plastics recycling, Reuters reported.

The judge rebuffed Bonta's claim that he was immune from being sued because he criticized Exxon (XOM) in his official capacity and in the "heartland" of his employment duties.

The judge also said whether the AG criticized Exxon (XOM) in good faith was a question of fact to be determined later.

Exxon (XOM) sued Bonta in January 2025, after California had filed a lawsuit accusing the company of engaging in decades of lies about the limitations of recycling.

The company has shown more willingness to fight back against environmentalists and other critics who say its operations harm the climate and add to greenhouse gas emissions.

data.title: Public Storage outlines PS4.0 transition and projects $16.35–$17 core FFO for 2026, with strategic leadership realignment
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Public Storage (PSA) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Joseph Russell announced a generational leadership transition with Tom Boyle promoted to CEO and Trustee, stating “Tom and I have been partners for nearly a decade since we both joined Public Storage in 2016. As you know, Tom has proven to be an exceptional leader in both his CFO and CIO roles with outstanding accomplishments across capital allocation, operations and financial strategy.” Russell also welcomed Joe Fisher as President and CFO, citing Fisher’s “tenure at UDR as President, CFO and CIO, along with his stature in the REIT industry.”
  • Russell highlighted the launch of PS4.0, emphasizing, “We're unveiling PS4.0, the next era of Public Storage leadership and strategy.” He noted that “From 2023 to 2025, Public Storage has led the sector in same-store revenue growth, NOI growth and NOI margins. Our core FFO per share growth leads the sector. And our total shareholder returns of 18.6% outperformed our peers over that time frame.”
  • Russell detailed Board changes, with Ron Havner stepping down as Chairman, John Reyes retiring, and Shankh Mitra of Welltower becoming Chairman. Both Mitra and Havner purchased $25 million and $5 million, respectively, in long-term options “demonstrating their commitment and confidence of what PS4.0 will deliver to shareholders.”
  • CFO and incoming CEO Tom Boyle described a strategic shift to the PS4.0 era, “This is a generational transition and a strategic vision designed to drive accelerated performance. As Joe said, our objective is simple: build the best team to attack the opportunity ahead, and we have.” Boyle also announced a headquarters move to Frisco, Texas and new leaders in key executive roles.
  • Joe Fisher, President and CFO, stated, “Core FFO in the quarter was $4.26 per share, resulting in full year core FFO of $16.97 per share at the high end of our guidance range. Same-store revenue and NOI growth in the quarter were minus 0.2% and minus 1.5%, respectively. Declines in move-in rents were offset by strong existing customer performance, resulting in in-place rents up 20 basis points and occupancy down 20 basis points.”

Outlook

  • The company established initial 2026 core FFO guidance in the range of $16.35 to $17, with a midpoint of $16.68, representing a year-over-year decline of 1.7%. Management noted, “Negative same-store NOI growth and refinancing activity is being offset by positive contributions from our non-same-store pool and our tenant insurance program.”
  • Same-store revenue and NOI guidance for 2026 are minus 1.1% and minus 2.2% at the midpoint, respectively. Fisher stated, “We believe occupancy for the year will remain roughly stable. Move-in rents will remain negative in the mid-single digits for the year, but will improve throughout the year, and our ECRI contribution will continue to help support total revenue.”
  • The company factored in a continued drag from the Los Angeles state of emergency, “resulting in a drag on same-store revenue of approximately 80 basis points.”

Financial Results

  • Core FFO for the quarter was $4.26 per share, with full year core FFO at $16.97 per share. Same-store revenue was down 0.2%, and same-store NOI was down 1.5% for the quarter. Expense growth for Q4 was 4.2%.
  • Non-same-store NOI growth was 20% for the quarter, driving core FFO per share higher by 1.2% year-over-year. The company reported $131 million in Q4 acquisitions and $953 million in total for 2025, with stabilized yields in the high 6% range. The development pipeline stands at $610 million, with $416 million unfunded.
  • The lending platform reached $142 million outstanding at a current rate of 7.9%. Available liquidity at quarter end was $1.8 billion, with annual free cash flow of approximately $600 million.

Q&A

  • Nicholas Joseph, Citigroup Inc.: Questioned near-term external growth opportunities and differences under PSA 4.0. Boyle explained, “We had a good number of single and double type opportunities... and we continue to try to capture as well as small and medium-sized portfolios... we're investing in the team. Our data science team has done tremendous work... streamlining our processes and looking to take advantage of the industry's largest data set.”
  • Eric Wolfe, Citigroup Inc.: Asked about same-store revenue guidance excluding Los Angeles and forward indicators. Fisher responded that “year-over-year revenue is a backward-looking indicator... occupancy for the year, we expect will be relatively static... new move-ins, we do forecast that while down mid-single digits for the year, we're going to start low and continue to lift throughout the year.”
  • Spenser Allaway, Green Street Advisors: Inquired about move-in rents and AI pricing strategy. Boyle noted, “Move-in rents for January... down 7%... sequential improvement... continued evolution there as we think about attracting the right customers at the top of funnel, being able to understand what we think their length of stays are going to be and their price elasticities.”
  • Multiple analysts focused on supply dynamics, capital allocation, expense containment, regulatory risk, and the impact of leadership changes. Management highlighted judicious development, ongoing digital investment, and a strengthened acquisition team.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised concerns about revenue headwinds, supply, and execution in a muted growth environment, often probing for clarity on guidance, risk, and strategic priorities. The tone was neutral to slightly cautious, with a focus on underlying trends and execution.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, emphasizing the strategic transition to PS4.0 and investments in talent and technology. In Q&A, responses were detailed and occasionally defensive, especially regarding regulatory risks and market headwinds. Confidence was expressed through phrases like “we are energized by the opportunity ahead.”
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the sentiment shifted from stabilization and incremental optimism to a more measured confidence, reflecting a strategic pivot and leadership change amid a more challenging operating environment.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Transition to new leadership (Boyle as CEO, Fisher as President and CFO) and Board changes mark a significant shift from the previous quarter’s continuity.
  • Introduction of PS4.0 and a new incentive structure indicate a strategic shift toward accelerated performance and team-based accountability.
  • Guidance changed from an upward revision in Q3 to a modest decline for 2026, with same-store NOI and revenue expected to be negative at the midpoint.
  • Analysts in both quarters maintained a probing tone, but the current quarter saw increased focus on external growth, capital allocation, and regulatory risks. Management’s tone in Q4 was more forward-looking but tempered by acknowledgment of headwinds.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited continued negative same-store NOI and revenue guidance, and refinancing activity as headwinds.
  • The Los Angeles state of emergency remains a drag, contributing an 80 basis point negative impact to same-store revenue.
  • Regulatory and legislative risks were highlighted, particularly around pricing transparency and disclosure requirements in California and other states. Boyle noted, “We're certainly aware of some of the recent pronouncements out of New York, for instance, and other states around pricing transparency... and making sure we're in compliance.”
  • Analysts expressed concerns about expense containment, supply reacceleration, top-of-funnel demand, and the impact of new compensation and incentive structures.

Final Takeaway

Public Storage is embarking on a new era with PS4.0, marked by a strategic leadership transition, a focus on capital allocation, and digital transformation. The company projects core FFO for 2026 in the range of $16.35 to $17 and is positioning itself for organic and external growth despite anticipated headwinds in same-store revenue and NOI. With a robust balance sheet, ongoing investments in AI and customer experience, and an expanded acquisition pipeline, Public Storage aims to drive long-term shareholder value while navigating regulatory and operational challenges in the self-storage sector.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: OPEC weighs resuming oil production increases starting in April - report
data.description:
OPEC+ is leaning towards resuming its monthly oil production increases from April, Reuters reported Friday, after having paused increases for Q1, as the group prepares for peak summer demand and price strength is bolstered by tensions over U.S.-Iran relations.

A resumption would allow Saudi Arabia and other OPEC+ members to regain market share as other members, such as Russia and Iran, see their own output under strain from Western sanctions, and production from Kazakhstan is restrained by a series of setbacks.

Eight OPEC+ producers - Saudi Arabia, Russia, the United Arab Emirates, Kazakhstan, Kuwait, Iraq, Algeria, and Oman - will vote on the matter during their March 1 meeting.

The eight members raised production quotas from April through December last year by ~2.9M bbl/day, but the cartel halted output hikes for the January-to-March period in an effort to curb supply glut fears amid the lower demand typically seen during winter.

OPEC's latest oil market forecast shows demand for OPEC+ crude in Q2 declining by 400K bbl/day from Q1, but it sees demand for the whole year coming in 600K bbl/day higher than in 2025 - a more optimistic view than most groups, including the International Energy Agency, which this week lowered its projection for global oil demand growth this year.

However, crude oil futures inched higher on Friday, although not enough to prevent oil's first back-to-back weekly drop this year.

Data released Friday showed U.S. consumer prices rose less than expected in January, up 0.2%, thanks to cheaper gasoline prices and a moderation in rental inflation.

"Inflation is stabilizing... a boon for interest rates to probably continue to move a little bit lower. And I think as rates start to move lower... that's a positive to the economy," BOK Financial senior VP of trading Dennis Kissler said in a note.

Reports of a second U.S. aircraft carrier heading to the Middle East keep the specter of eventual military action in sight, Kissler said.

"Near-term global crude supplies remain ample and crude futures are likely holding a $5-$7/bbl geopolitical premium," Kissler wrote, adding that U.S. negotiations with Iran and Russia will be near-term market movers.

Also on Friday, the U.S. Treasury Department issued a general license officially allowing Chevron (CVX) and some European firms - Shell (SHEL), BP (BP), Eni (E) and Repsol (REPYF) (REPYY) - to invest in new oil and gas operations in Venezuela; those companies have maintained offices and stakes in various projects in the country.

Front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for March delivery closed +0.1% on Friday to $62.89/bbl, and front-month Brent crude (CO1:COM) for April delivery ended +0.3% to $67.75/bbl; for the week, Nymex and Brent gained 1% and 0.4%, respectively.

Front-month Nymex natural gas (NG1:COM) for March delivery rose 0.8% for the day but fell 5.2% for the week to $3.243/MMBtu.

ETFs: (USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG), (XLE)

data.title: Westwood outlines $450M new client mandate and ETF growth amid $1B AUM outflows
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Westwood Holdings Group, Inc. (WHG) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Brian Casey highlighted several milestones, stating the ETF franchise now exceeds $200 million, including the launch of the Enhanced Income Opportunity ETF, while MDST surpassed $170 million in AUM. He noted, "We closed our second oversubscribed private equity fund, Westwood Energy Secondaries Fund II with more than $300 million in commitments for the fund and two related co-investment funds. Our managed investment solutions team secured its first institutional client, and we had strong full year sales growth. $2.5 billion versus $2.1 billion, up 20%."
  • Casey reported, "Institutional channel achieved gross sales growth of 36% versus the previous year," with particular traction in SMidCap and SmallCap value strategies.
  • He emphasized product expansion, sharing, "The infrastructure and liquid real asset strategies we launched last year have attracted strong interest from institutional investors seeking alternatives to traditional equity and fixed income allocations."
  • On the wealth division, Casey described a "multiyear evolution" focused on scalability and client experience, and expressed, "We remain focused on enhancing the client experience, improving scalability and positioning our business for sustainable long-term growth."
  • On ETF progress, Casey stated, "Our MDST ETF has now achieved the asset scale required for approval on major broker-dealer platforms and we expect new platform additions this year."
  • CFO Murray Forbes reported, "Today, we reported total revenues of $27.1 million for the fourth quarter of 2025 compared to $24.3 million in the third quarter and $25.6 million in the prior year's fourth quarter."
  • Forbes added, "Our fourth quarter income of $1.9 million or $0.21 per share compared to the third quarter's $3.7 million or $0.41 per share due to higher performance-related incentive compensation in the fourth quarter and unrealized depreciation on strategic private investment in the third quarter, offset by higher revenues."

Outlook

  • Casey projected ongoing "market uncertainty driven by a variety of economic indicators and policy developments," but maintained confidence that Westwood's "focus on high-quality businesses with strong fundamentals positions us well for the future."
  • He revealed, "We did have a new client come in yesterday with $200 million, and they will add another $100 million to $200 million over the next couple of months. We also have a new defined contribution plan that will fund on the last day of the first quarter in our SMid product for $450 million."
  • Casey said, "Our pipeline looks great. We have -- we reached another new threshold last night where MDST our midstream enhanced energy income fund crossed the $200 million threshold."

Financial Results

  • Forbes stated, "For fiscal 2025, total revenues of $97.8 million compared to $94.7 million in 2024 driven by higher average assets under management and higher revenues from our ETFs and private energy secondaries funds."
  • Forbes detailed, "Economic earnings for the year were $14.3 million or $1.61 per share compared with $7 million or $0.82 per share in 2024."
  • Firm-wide assets under management and advisement totaled $17.4 billion at quarter end, consisting of $16.5 billion in AUM and $0.9 billion in assets under advisement.
  • Forbes explained, "Over the year, our assets under management experienced net outflows of $1 billion and market appreciation of $1 billion and our assets under advisement experienced net outflows of $18 million."
  • Forbes reported, "Our financial position continues to be very solid, with cash and liquid investments at quarter end totaling $44.1 million and a debt-free balance sheet."
  • Casey disclosed, "Our Board of Directors approved a regular cash dividend of $0.15 per common share payable on April 1, 2026."

Q&A

  • No analyst questions were recorded in the Q&A session. The operator closed the Q&A, returning the call to Casey, who addressed outflows and the outlook: "We felt like we had a really good year in 2025, but we want to acknowledge the outflows in the fourth quarter, which were disappointing. More than 80% of the outflows were from our large-cap value product...most of those large cap outflows, in fact, more than 80% of those flows were from one sub-advisory client that carries a fee of less than 20 basis points."
  • Casey followed, "We did have a new client come in yesterday with $200 million, and they will add another $100 million to $200 million over the next couple of months."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts did not participate in the Q&A, so their sentiment was not captured.
  • Management maintained a confident and proactive tone in prepared remarks, but acknowledged disappointment regarding outflows. Casey said, "We felt like we had a really good year in 2025, but we want to acknowledge the outflows in the fourth quarter, which were disappointing."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone shifted from cautiously optimistic with a focus on growth, to a more measured optimism coupled with candid recognition of outflows and their limited revenue impact.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Revenue increased to $27.1 million in Q4 from $24.3 million in Q3, while income dropped to $1.9 million from $3.7 million, attributed to higher performance-related incentive compensation.
  • Assets under management decreased to $16.5 billion from $17.3 billion, with net outflows of $1 billion in Q4 versus $0.7 billion in Q3.
  • Management highlighted strong pipeline prospects, including a $450 million mandate, and continued ETF growth, consistent with previous quarter commentary but with greater emphasis on addressing outflows.
  • The previous quarter’s call noted disappointment in outflows, but this quarter provided additional detail about their source and revenue impact, reflecting a more transparent approach.
  • Analyst participation was lower this quarter compared to the previous quarter’s Q&A.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited "outflows in the fourth quarter," particularly from a large-cap value product, as a key concern, but emphasized the low fee impact on revenue.
  • Casey indicated efforts to mitigate outflows by pointing to new client wins and funding commitments, as well as a robust pipeline and anticipated platform additions for ETFs.

Final Takeaway

Westwood Holdings Group closed 2025 with notable growth in its ETF franchise, strong institutional sales, and new fund launches, while addressing a challenging quarter characterized by significant outflows from a large-cap value product. Management underscored the lower revenue impact of these outflows and pointed to robust pipeline opportunities, including a $450 million SMid product mandate and further ETF platform expansion. The company remains confident in its active management strategy and distribution momentum, supported by a solid balance sheet and continued dividend payments.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Agnico Eagle plans 20%–30% decade-long production growth as cash flow, reserves reach record highs
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Agnico Eagle Mines Limited (AEM) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Ammar Al-Joundi highlighted, "In 2025, as gold prices hit new highs throughout the year, Agnico Eagle delivered on our production targets, we delivered on our costs, and we did it responsibly and reliably." He emphasized that cash costs increased only $76 per ounce compared to a $1,700 rise in the gold price, stating, "We delivered over 95% of this gold price increase to the benefit of our shareholders, delivering on our core mandate of providing gold upside leverage to our owners."
  • Al-Joundi reported, "We repaid almost $1 billion in debt. We built up almost $3 billion in cash, and we returned over $1.4 billion directly to our owners through dividends and share buybacks, all while continuing to invest heavily in our future through the largest exploration budget we've ever had and through continued strong investments into our 5 key growth projects."
  • The CEO revealed Agnico Eagle's plan to increase production by up to 20% to 30% over the next decade, with a "path to over 4 million ounces of annual production by the early 2030s."
  • Projects at Detour Lake, Canadian Malartic, Upper Beaver, and Hope Bay are being accelerated, with significant capital increases and earlier production targets. For instance, investment at Detour Lake is tripling to $300 million, aiming for underground production as early as 2028, and Upper Beaver investment is rising to $300 million to support production by 2030.
  • CFO James Porter stated, "We finished the year with a solid fourth quarter, producing approximately 841,000 ounces of gold at total cash cost of $1,089 and all-in sustaining costs of $1,517 per ounce."
  • Porter reported, "We delivered a number of financial records in the fourth quarter, including record adjusted earnings of approximately $1.4 billion or $2.70 per share and record free cash flow of over $1.3 billion or $2.62 per share."
  • Porter also announced, "We increased the quarterly dividend by 12.5% to $0.45 per share. And at current gold prices, we expect to be more active on share buybacks. To support this, we intend to renew our normal course issuer bid in May and increase the purchase limit up to $2 billion."

Outlook

  • Management reiterated a stable annual production profile between 3.3 million and 3.5 million ounces over the next three years, with 2026 cash costs forecast to be up a little over $100 per ounce compared to last year, largely due to higher royalties and a stronger Canadian dollar.
  • Porter said, "The midpoint of our 2026 guidance ranges are $1,070 per ounce for cash costs and $1,475 per ounce for all-in sustaining costs. Approximately 60% of the increase in cash costs relative to 2025 reflects higher royalties, driven by a higher budgeted gold price of $4,500 per ounce and the impact of a stronger Canadian dollar."
  • Porter outlined, "We see the potential to increase [shareholder returns] to 40% or higher this year with flexibility depending on the gold price and the needs of the business."
  • The company expects to maintain capital expenditures at an elevated level as it accelerates growth projects, with Hope Bay potentially adding $300 million to 2026 capex if approved in May.

Financial Results

  • Porter reported, "For the full 2025 year, we exceeded the midpoint of our guidance with gold production of 3.45 million ounces."
  • Total cash costs and all-in sustaining costs for 2025 were $979 and $1,339 per ounce, respectively, both slightly above guidance due to higher royalty costs. He noted, "If we exclude the impact of higher royalties, our total cash cost would have been $937 per ounce, $42 per ounce lower and below the midpoint of our guidance."
  • Agnico Eagle reported record free cash flow of approximately $4.4 billion for the year, repaid approximately $950 million in debt, and ended 2025 with $2.9 billion in cash.
  • Shareholder returns through share buybacks and dividends totaled $500 million in Q4 and a record $1.4 billion for 2025.

Q&A

  • Lawson Winder, BofA Securities, Research Division: Asked about M&A activity and whether Agnico would tender shares to an offer on Foran. CEO Al-Joundi responded, "That's not really something I would be comfortable discussing," and elaborated that the company's focus for M&A is always on value per share, stating, "We are willing to move and we have moved when we see an opportunity on the M&A side that actually creates value per share."
  • Fahad Tariq, Jefferies: Inquired about cost productivity initiatives and their inclusion in 2026 ASIC guidance. Dominique Girard replied, "It's partially included, but not all...we keep some flexibility in that."
  • Joshua Wolfson, RBC Capital Markets: Questioned future capex trends with project ramp-ups. Porter said, "We will see capital kind of stay at that elevated level. And then once we start to see that stair step increase in production in 2030, you'd expect the capital to start to come off."
  • Daniel Major, UBS: Asked about cost estimates for Meadowbank life extension ounces. Girard confirmed, "The last number I saw was sort of in the $2,200 to $2,300" (ASIC).
  • Anita Soni, CIBC: Queried dividend policy sensitivity to gold price changes. Porter responded, "The reality is the gold price could pretty well be cut in half, and we'd be okay maintaining that level of dividend."
  • Tanya Jakusconek, Scotiabank: Sought confirmation of capital needs for project growth through 2031. Porter estimated, "$5 billion to $6 billion of growth spending over the course of '26 through 2030, I think is about the right estimate."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed management on capital allocation, project execution, and cost inflation, with a neutral to slightly positive tone, frequently congratulating the company on results but seeking clarity on guidance and capital returns.
  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone in both prepared remarks and responses, frequently using phrases such as "We are confident..." and "We are exceptionally well positioned."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts' tone shifted from an emphasis on productivity and cost control to more probing questions on capex, M&A, and capital returns, while management's confidence increased, supported by record results.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • In Q4, management unveiled a decade-long growth vision targeting over 4 million ounces of annual production by the early 2030s, a notable forward shift from Q3’s focus on ongoing project execution.
  • Dividend growth and share buyback expansion were announced, contrasting with Q3’s commentary on maintaining capital returns.
  • Capex guidance increased with more aggressive project acceleration, while Q3 focused on optimization and productivity improvements.
  • Analyst focus this quarter shifted toward long-term growth, capex needs, and capital allocation, versus prior quarter’s emphasis on productivity and near-term cost control.
  • Management’s tone was more assertive and optimistic, citing record cash, reserves, and resources, and outlining bolder growth targets.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged higher costs for 2026, primarily due to royalties and currency impacts, and highlighted ongoing inflationary pressures on labor and consumables, with labor at "about 45% of our overall cost structure."
  • The company faces elevated capex as it accelerates key growth projects, with risk mitigated through staged investments and maintaining a strong balance sheet.
  • Analysts raised questions about technical and human capital constraints as project activity ramps up, but management expressed comfort with current capacity and flexibility to adjust project timelines.

Final Takeaway

Agnico Eagle delivered on its 2025 commitments with record production, reserves, cash flow, and shareholder returns, while laying out an ambitious plan to grow production by up to 30% over the next decade. Management signaled continued discipline in capital allocation, a willingness to accelerate high-return projects, and a focus on delivering value per share. The company stands in its strongest financial position ever and expects to maintain industry-leading costs and robust shareholder returns as it pursues its next phase of growth.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
